description.value:
Source: Ariadne's Book of Legends - Alpha v1.2, p. 23-25.

Becoming Legendary

There are three aspects of play that you must consider when getting ready to embody a Legendary Character.

The World

What does it mean to be a Legend? At its core, the only fundamental behind such characters is their power relative to your regular 5e or 5.5e campaign. Though we’ve talked about tales and myths, about gathering magical artifacts and reaching heights yet unknown, a Legendary Character can be whatever you, or your world, decides.

Perhaps your campaign begins in an interstellar prison designed to keep even Ascended-level threats under strict surveillance–your Legend starts with the clothes on their back, and nothing more. Perhaps the world you inhabit is filled to the brim with powerful creatures, and though you are considerably powerful, there are legions composed entirely of 20th-level warriors across the cosmos, making you a small fish in the grand scheme. Or perhaps you are a living myth, the last of a long-lost race of demigods, only challenged by the greatest forces the universe has to offer. The scope of the story will determine a lot of what being Legendary actually is.

In most cases, you can expect a balance between these extremes; a setting similar to that of a comic book or an anime, in which power levels continue to rise beyond what D&D has to offer without it seeming contrived. Know the world your Legend inhabits and respect it–just because you are at 20th level doesn’t mean your character is exempt from logic.

Breaking The Game. Running a Legendary Campaign is a hard thing, and your DM may not be as experienced with high-level D&D as you are. You might have seen broken combos on the internet and are dying to try them out, such as an infinite army of constructs created over the course of a year, a glyph of warding nuclear bomb, or even a magic item interaction that can instantly delete an enemy–and this is before accounting for the Legendary rules introduced in this expansion.

Chances are, your DM will, at some point, create a broken interaction that is clearly not working as intended. Let them know, and don’t try to use what are clear oversights to beat the game. The DM is trying to create a fun experience for your table–using exploits to push the game past its limits makes the DM’s job harder, and ultimately makes the table less enjoyable for everyone.

This is not to say you should avoid doing legendary stuff, but with great power comes great responsibility. It is on you to communicate your intentions clearly and work with your DM, not behind their back. When you notice something that might be problematic, find a moment to tell them. Together, you might find that it can be left in the game without much issue! Most of the time, bringing it to your DM’s attention is enough for them to plan around it.

Nazareno (playing as Astrid the Wizard): Duke Placeholderton is clearly corrupt. I’d like to cast scrying on him, wait for him to be alone, then teleport into his chambers, capture him in a demiplane, replace him with a simulacrum, and have the clone sign the treaty.

DM: Astrid certainly has the power to do that. But… the duke’s manor sits in the middle of a city of 40 thousand people who know and love him. Word will travel fast when their lord starts acting strange.

Nazareno: Fair points. Astrid thinks for a moment–she’s lived long enough to know that the quick solution might not be the best. She requests an audience with the Duke’s assistant.

DM: Now that’s interesting. The duke receives you personally. He’s heard of Astrid the Planeswalker. He looks cautious. Scared, perhaps.

Manuel (playing as Manhuman the Fighter): Manhuman stands by the door with his arms crossed. He’s not saying anything. He just stands there, menacingly.

DM: The duke keeps glancing at Manhuman. He might sign the treaty after all…

Manuel: Good.

DM. …but not before raising an objection with you. “See, there’s this Lich by the name of Xerias” he begins. It seems he’s being intimidated by this Dark Lord, who does not want this treaty to take effect.

Nazareno. Finally! A clue! “We’re hunting this Lich, Grand Duke. Tell us what you know. We’ll guarantee your safety.”

Manuel: Manhuman nods. It’s a powerful nod.

The Roleplay

A Legendary Character is not simply a more powerful version of who they were at 5th level. Years of adventure, loss, triumph, and transformation have shaped them into something categorically different. The way your character carries themselves, the way they speak to kings and Gods, the poise they have for decisions that would paralyze a lesser hero–these are the factors of Legendary roleplay.

This does not mean your character has to be stoic, infallible, or above it all. Legends are defined as much by their flaws and obsessions as by their virtues. A 20thlevel barbarian who still flies into a rage when someone insults their homeland is not playing their character wrong! Power does not erase who a person is. If anything, it amplifies it.

What does change is scale. The problems a Legendary Character concerns itself with are not the same problems that occupied them at earlier levels. A dispute between two merchants is beneath their notice, unless they have a weird thing for the fairness of bartering. The collapse of a planar boundary, the rise of a Threadbearer, the fate of an entire civilization – these are the stakes that feel appropriate. Let that inform how your character moves through the world, what they choose to engage with, and what they leave to others.

Of course, you can always subvert expectations; your extremely powerful fighter might be obsessed with helping out every grandma cross the street, your wizard could be unable to ignore a certain monster infestation, your cleric may stop at every church the party comes across–just make sure these are quick additions that don’t deter from the main story being told (which most likely does concern itself with the fate of the cosmos!).

Power. Consider your character’s relationship with their own power. Some Legends wear it lightly, treating godlike capability as simply what they are. Others carry it as a burden, aware of what they could do and careful about it. Talk to your DM and your fellow players about what kind of story you want to tell at this tier. A Legendary campaign works best when everyone at the table understands the genre they’re in.

Morality. The relativity of morals is a theme exacerbated by the might of legends–if law cannot hold you accountable, what rules will you heed?

If you choose to follow the tenets of your society, you may become the greatest defender of their traditions and the maker of new ones. If you perform acts of good, your sheer power could inspire thousands to behave nobly. If, on the other hand, you lean more into the villainous side, you could be hunted down by adventuring parties looking to end your rampage of terror. Your actions are informed by a sense of right and wrong; that balance has an oversized effect upon the world, owing to your influence as a living myth.

Side Quests. Legendary Characters can treat most non-Legendary adventures as a simple 5-minute ordeal.

Teleport in, slay the bad guy in one surprise round, and return to the safety of your fort. These are quick side quests that can add a lot of flavor to your roleplay.

John (playing as Elizabeth the Cleric): While we’re staying in this city, can I stop by the churches for a few hours every day and heal the sick?

DM: Of course! Your Divine Touch can lift curses, heal wounds, and even regenerate missing limbs. The people adore you, clamoring to have their young ones blessed by your touch, even if nothing’s wrong with them. You begin to gather somewhat of a cult.

John. Oh, Elizabeth doesn’t like that one bit. If the crowds ever get too overwhelming, I’ll use my Proclamation to calm and disperse them.

DM. You speak a divine word, and the clouds in the sky part to let the sun illuminate you as you pass. People move with your step, in awe of your divine gift. They won’t bother you, but they might erect a statue of you…

John. Elizabeth gives them a thumbs up as she heads back into the noble district!

The Gameplay

As mentioned previously, the book presents five new levels beyond the 20th for each class, along with some general adjustments to level 20. As you level up, you will gain access to additional class features that build upon those you already know, while introducing three entirely new additions to the game: Heroic Paths, Mythical Techniques, and 10th-level Spells. These interact with the Legendary Point System detailed in the introduction.

Outside of legendary additions, characters keep increasing their hit points as usual, and increase their proficiency bonus by 1 at levels 22 and 24, when changing Tiers of Play. Other minor improvements, such as additional spell slots, increased rage or sneak attack damage, and dice improvements, are detailed in each class’s legendary leveling table.

Legendary Points. Legendary Points are your primary resource at this tier of play, fueling your most powerful abilities and your defenses alike. Managing them well is what separates a character who thrives in these battles from one who is overwhelmed by them.

Heroic Paths. With each Legendary Level, you will gain a Heroic Perk, a point you can use to advance a Heroic Path of your choosing. Each Heroic Path has its own prerequisites, and some of them connect, allowing you to create a unique legend. Think of Heroic Paths as a continuation of your subclass, along with a progression system similar to Epic Boons. Each step of your Heroic Path will unlock several features, much like a skill tree, and you can mix and match perks from different paths to discover your very own character combos.

You can find Heroic Paths detailed in Chapter II of this book.

Mythical Techniques. Martials and Half-Casters will gain access to Mythical Techniques. These are powerful actions of varying cost that allow for incredible feats of strength. Some may be passive abilities, while others might require an expenditure of Legendary Points, and others still can only be used once per day.

At different Legendary Levels, depending on your class, you will be able to choose a Mythical Technique. These abilities are separated by Mythical Schools, much like spells, and are tailored to specific classes.

You can find Mythical Techniques detailed in Chapter III of this book.

Epic Magic. Wish is no longer the mightiest spell a mortal can cast. There are others. 10th-level spells are the greatest effects both Characters and Foes can create during gameplay. They require a great investment of Legendary Points, a 10th-level spell slot, and the right circumstances to capitalize on them. They are a party’s greatest strength, and only come into play at the Paragon-tier of play, meaning the jump from level

21 to level 22 is perhaps the biggest single boost of power you will receive in your Legendary Campaign.

You can find Epic Magic detailed in Chapter IV of this book.

Relics. Magic items change how some characters play, making up for key weaknesses or improving their most useful capabilities. A +3 weapon is almost mandatory in this tier of play, along with utility objects that may help position the party during battle. There is, however, treasure greater even than the mighty Artifacts; Relics, world-changing items that are connected to the incredible foes presented in this book and their storylines. Because of their sheer power, Relics take up 2 attunement slots instead of one.

Wielding a Relic is no easy task, with many previous requirements and a long attunement process. Once a character has successfully bonded with a Relic, they increase in power substantially, some even granting special Legendary Levels.

You can find Relics detailed in Chapter VII of this book.

Mercedes (playing as Ellie the Warlock). How many Legendary Points does he have left after that, DM?

DM: The Hermit used 1 LP to hurl a star at you during his turn, and he just used 2 LP to cast Prismatic Wall, so he’s fairly spent. Still has 2 LP for the rest of the round.

Mercedes: Alright, I’ll use an LP to fire a Legendary Blast at him and take cover behind the floating ruin there. That’s.. 140 points of force damage, DM!

DM: Very well. Onto your turn, Alvar. What does Alesthesian do?

Alvar (playing as Alesthesian the Druid). I’ll fly toward Rekhar in my Solar form and throw him towards the hermit.

DM: And Rekhar is holding that Divine Severance for 2 LP, right?

Hans (playing as Rehkar the Monk): Oh yeah.

DM: I see; good! Alesthesian flies 150 feet up and throws you towards the Hermit. You loosen your mythical technique, and a crimson burst of power escapes your hands and becomes a wound of unendurable energy. The Hermit rolls the save… and fails. It will use 1 LP to succeed on that with a Legendary Resistance, though he still takes half damage. His protective barrier is gone.

Mercedes: OK, no more Legendary Resistance for the round!

Julian (playing as Mazdamundi the Sorcerer). And no barrier either. It’s time. I’ll use 2 LP to cast my 10th-level spell, Theophrastus’ Flux. I’ll say, “Even stars can turn to dust” as the transmutation burst escapes my fingers!

source.custom: Original en-US text from Alpha v1.2
